coredata.dc:title: High photoluminescence nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots for assessment of microbial viability
coredata.dc:creator: Qie, Xingwang
Zan, Minghui
Li, Li
Gui, Ping
Chang, Zhimin
Ge, Mingfeng
Wang, Ruo-Song
Guo, Zhenzhen
Dong, Wen-Fei
coredata.dc:description: Assessment of microbial viability plays a key role in human health protection. Optical imaging based on fluorescent dyes is a simple and convenient way to assess microbial viability. However, it is still a challenge to obtain stable, nontoxic and low-cost dyes. Herein, we prepared a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots (N, P-CDs) via a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared CDs possess plenty of functional groups and exhibit high stability, good biocompatibility, excellent photoluminescent and low toxicity. Especially, the properties of high quantum yield (89.9%) and highly negative surface charge (−41.9 mV) make the prepared N, P-CDs ideal materials for microbial differentiation. Compared with commercial dyes, our CDs are more stable, cost less, which can rapidly distinguish dead microorganisms from living ones with higher specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Highly negative surface charge
Microbial viability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and fungi
Comparison between N, P-CDs and PI on live and dead microbial detection
Comparison between N, P-CDs and PI on living and dead microbial detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFF0108600 , 2017YFC0211900 and 2016YFF0103800 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81771982 , 61535010 , 8160071152 and 21803075 ), Key Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KFZD-SW-210 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2015601 , BK20181236 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the synthetic route and selective dead bacterial staining of N, P-CDs. (B) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM image) of N, P-CDs. (C) Dynamic light scattering spectrum of the CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of N, P-CDs and (B–D) the high-resolution XPS peaks of C 1s, N 1s and P 2p, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence excitation spectrum of N, P-CDs. The insets are photographs taken under daylight (left) and a 365 nm UV lamp (right), respectively. (B) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs at different excitation wavelengths under ambient conditions.

Bright-field and fluorescence images of live/dead bacteria including three Gram-positive bacteria (M. luteus, B. subtilis and S. aureus) and three Gram-negative bacteria (E. coil, S. typhimurium and K. pneumoniae) stained with N, P-CDs. Fluorescence images were captured under the excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 552 nm, respectively. (Scale bar: 25 μm).

Bright-field and fluorescence images of live/dead fungi including S. cerevisiae and A. niger stained with N, P-CDs. Fluorescence images were captured under the excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 552 nm, respectively. (Scale bar: 25 μm).
Confocal images of live/dead S. aureus and S. cerevisiae treated by N, P-CDs and PI, respectively. (Scale bar: 25 μm).

head.ce:title: High photoluminescence nitrogen, phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots for assessment of microbial viability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Leibniz-Institut für Polymerforschung Dresden e.V., Institute of Physical Chemistry and Polymer Physics, Hohe Straße 6, 01069, Dresden, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical Chemistry and Polymer Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Leibniz-Institut für Polymerforschung Dresden e.V., Institute of Physical Chemistry and Polymer Physics, Hohe Straße 6, 01,069 Dresden, Germany

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Assessment of microbial viability plays a key role in human health protection. Optical imaging based on fluorescent dyes is a simple and convenient way to assess microbial viability. However, it is still a challenge to obtain stable, nontoxic and low-cost dyes. Herein, we prepared a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots (N, P-CDs) via a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared CDs possess plenty of functional groups and exhibit high stability, good biocompatibility, excellent photoluminescent and low toxicity. Especially, the properties of high quantum yield (89.9%) and highly negative surface charge (−41.9 mV) make the prepared N, P-CDs ideal materials for microbial differentiation. Compared with commercial dyes, our CDs are more stable, cost less, which can rapidly distinguish dead microorganisms from living ones with higher specificity.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial infections seriously threaten public health [1,2]. For this reason, microorganism detection is very important in human health protection [3–5]. Numerous techniques such as AFM (atomic force microscopy), NASBA (nucleic acid sequence-based amplification), SERS (surface-enhanced Roman scattering), FTIR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) and PCR (polymerase chain reaction) have been developed to differentiate live and dead microbial cells. Although these methods are workable, they are usually time-consuming, costly and require complicated procedures [6–9]. To address these issues, optical imaging based on fluorescent dyes is most frequently used in microbial differentiation owing to their simple operations, short duration and visualization [10–13]. Lots of fluorescent probes have been developed such as propidium iodide (PI) [14], 4, 6-di-amidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and Rhodamine 123 [15,16]. However, these fluorescent probes are always expensive, highly toxic, difficult to store, and unstable for its photobleaching under the laser. Therefore, it is very significant to search a high-quantity, bio-safe and cost-effective material for microbial viability detection.

In recent years, carbon nanodots (CDs) have been regarded as promising fluorescent materials for their superior optical and chemical properties [17–19]. Low cost, excellent biocompatibility, multiple functional groups, superior photostability make them star materials in bioimaging, detection, nanomedicine, biosensing and catalysis [20–22]. To further enhance the properties of CDs, excellent researchers developed the methods of heteroatom doping in which the CDs can be surface functionalized more easily. Doping elements in CDs can provide extra long pairs of elements or virtual orbital [8]. To obtain heteroatom doped CDs, a great variety of methods were developed. Nitrogen, sulfur and phosphorus are mostly used to enhance the electronic and optical properties for higher quantum yield, longer emission wavelength and much more functional groups, which make CDs wonderful candidates for detecting living and dead microorganisms [23–27].

Hence, the N, P-CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method by using 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid, urea and H3PO4. To test the performance of N, P-CDs in microbial viability assessment, we prepared six cell types of bacteria cells (three G + bacteria and three G- bacteria) and two cell types of fungi. We found that the prepared CDs can selectively recognize dead bacteria and fungi rather than living ones. We speculated that the particular surface functional groups and highly negative surface charge facilitate the penetration of CDs through damaged cell membranes specifically. With the doping of nitrogen and phosphorus, the CDs have a very high quantum yield (89.9%). Compared with the traditional dyes, our CDs are more stable, cost less, and they can achieve an accurate assessment of microbial viability rapidly with multi-color imaging.

1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid and urea were purchased from Energy Reagent Company. Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were bought from Aladdin. Throughout the experiments, all the chemical reagents were not further purified and the water was deionized before used. The HeLa cell line was obtained from Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Science. The bacterial cells and fungal cells were acquired from the China General Microbiological Culture Collection (CGMCC).

The N, P-CDs were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method. 0.2 g 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid and 0.4 g urea were dissolved into 20 mL H3PO4. The mixture was then transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-line autoclave. After heating at 180 °C for 10 h, the solution was cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the tawny liquid was centrifuged at 5000 rpm and dialyzed with a semipermeable membrane (MWCO 1000) for 24 h to remove the unreactive molecules. Finally, the powder was acquired with the method of freeze-drying for the subsequent experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were obtained on a JEOL-2100 F transmission electron microscope. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were measured by a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano ZS90). The UV–vis absorption was recorded on UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 300 Scan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a Bruker Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Hyperion). The fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum of the carbon nanodots was characterized by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AXIS ULTRA DLD).

Quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4; relative QY ∼ 0.54) was chosen as a reference to measure the quantum yield of CDs. The QY of CDs was calculated according to the equation:
ФX=ФXT(AXT/AX)(IX/IXT)(nX/nXT)2
Where “Ф” is the QY of CDs; “A” represents the optical density (absorption); “I” refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity; “n” is the refractive index. The subscript “X” stands the carbon dots and “XT” stands the standard, respectively. To achieve better results, the absorption of CDs and referenced samples were adjusted below 0.10 (fluorescence cuvette).

To test the cytotoxicity of CDs, a 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed with HeLa cells cultured in atmosphere at 37 °C. The HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at the density of 5000 cells per well. After culturing for 24 h, a series of concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 μg mL−1) of CDs were added to the cells and incubated for another 12 h. Next, we added 15 μL MTT (5 mg mL) into each well and inculcated for 4 h. Subsequently, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was injected into each well to dissolve the formazan. Finally, we recorded the absorption of the mixture with a microplate reader at the wavelength of 570 nm after shaking for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and fungi
ce:section.ce:para: Three cell types of G + bacterial cells (B. subtilies, S. aureus and M. luteus) and three cell types of G- bacterial cells (E. coli, S. typhimurium and M. luteus) and two types of fungi (S. cerevisiae and A. niger) were selected to test the performance of living and dead cells recognition of N, P-CDs. Bacterial cells were cultured in lysogeny broth (LB) medium and fungal cells were cultured in potato-dextrose broth medium. All cells were incubated at 37 °C at 180 rpm via shaking overnight. Then the cells were then centrifuged in aseptic PBS at 8000 rpm thrice. To obtain dead cells, the live ones we boiled at 80 °C for 1 h. Both live and dead cells were dispersed in N, P-CDs solution (200 μg mL−1) and cultured at 37 °C for 3 h. To remove the CDs that did not enter the cells, the microorganism were centrifuged again at 8000 rpm for three times. The CLSM images were acquired with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 552 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between N, P-CDs and PI on live and dead microbial detection
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the microbial live/dead detection performance between N, P-CDs and PI (a commercial dye), living and dead (boiled at 80 °C for 1 h) cells including S. aureus and S. cerevisiae were chosen and mixed in the proportion of 1:1. And the bacteria were treated with both N, P-CDs (200 μg mL−1) and PI (30 μM) at 37 °C for 2 h. Before the CLSM analysis, the cells were washed three times at 8000 rpm to remove the dye outside the cells. The excitation and emission wavelength is 535 and 615 nm, respectively. The image conditions were optimized to avoid the special overlap between PI and CDs. The yeast was also been stained with CDs (200 μg mL−1) and PI (30 μM) to validate the live/dead differentiation performance of N, P-CDs on fungal cells.

N, P-doped CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal treatment as described in Fig. 1 A. The morphologies of N, P-CDs were characterized through both TEM and DLS. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the prepared CDs were well-dispersed with a mean partial diameter of 3.5 nm. The crystalline structure can be observed from the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of N, P-CDs (Fig. 1B inset). The lattice spacing of CDs was 0.24 nm, which is close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon. In addition, a narrow size distribution from 3 to 6 nm, shown in DLS (Fig. 1C), is remarkably consistent with the TEM image.

The surface groups and chemical components of the N, P-CDs were investigated by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 2 showed the FTIR spectrum of the CDs, the two broad absorption peaks around 3410 cm−1 and 2917 cm−1 are attributed to OH and NH, respectively. These small peaks at 1485 cm−1, 1347 cm−1, 1233 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 are assigned to CO, CN, PO and CO stretching vibrations, separately. The band at 890 cm−1 is identified as the stretching vibrations of PC and PN. All the above active surface groups indicated as N and P are highly doped in our CDs. XPS was employed to analyze the chemical composition content and surface group more comprehensively. The major elements detected in N, P-CDs are C (73.55%), N (8.63%), O (12.4%) and P (5.42%), which is consistent with the FTIR analysis (Fig. 3 A). The XPS spectrum of C 1s shows four peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.7 eV, corresponding to CC, CC/CN/CP, CO and OCO, respectively (Fig. 3B). The XPS N 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.4 eV, accordingly and are attributed to NH and (C)3 N (Fig. 3C). Also, the XPS spectrum of P 2p contains two components that can be assigned to CP (133.4 eV) and PO (134.1 eV) (Fig. 3D). Those structural characterizations suggest that the successful doping of N and P in our CDs again.

Further researches demonstrated that the N, P-CDs have satisfied optical properties. The CDs have high solubility in aqueous media, and can also emit strong blue fluorescence with the irradiation by 365 nm UV ray (Fig. 4 A Insets). The normalized fluorescent spectrum about absorption and excitation of N, P-CDs are shown in Fig. 4A. The absorption spectrum has an absorption peak at 220 nm, which can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC. The doping oxygen and nitrogen elements serve as surface defects on the surface of CDs, which are responsible for fluorescence emission [28,29]. Fig. 4B shows the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs with different wavelengths of excitation light, and the spectra exhibit characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescent feature with optimal emission at 445 nm under the 310 nm excitation. This emission property is caused by various surface states and different energy levels due to the distinct functional groups of CDs [30,31].

To study the impact of reaction ratio and solution on quantum yield (QY), the control experiments with different reaction ratio and in different solvents including alcohol, water and phosphoric acid were carried out. As shown in the results (Tables S1 and S2), we obtained a higher QY of 83% with a reaction ratio of 2:1 in phosphoric acid solution. All these advantages make N, P-CDs a promising fluorescent probe in bioimaging.

Cytotoxicity analysis of N, P-CDs was performed via MTT assay in HeLa cell line model. HeLa cells were cultured with a series of concentrations of CDs (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 μg mL) for 12 h. As shown in Fig. S1, the cell viability remains to be more than 85%, even at the highest concentration of N, P-CDs (350 μg mL). The results demonstrated that the N, P-CDs exhibited extremely low cytotoxicity under the experimental conditions. Therefore, those prepared CDs will have great potential in the applications of bio-sensing, biomedical imaging and medical diagnosis.

For practical applications, it is necessary for N, P-CDs to have good fluorescent stability under various conditions. For this reason, we have investigated the fluorescent stability of N, P-CDs with different temperatures, UV irradiation time, pH value and ionic strength, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S2B, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs remains almost constant with pH value ranging from 3 to 10. And a slight decrease was shown with the increasing of temperature (Fig. S2A) and UV irradiation time (Fig. S2C). In addition, there is also no obvious photobleaching under different ionic strength (Fig. S2D). These results fully indicate that the N, P-CDs have remarkable photostability and great propects for biological development.

Since the negatively charged nature of bacterial cells, the bacterial-derived carbon dots should have a negatively charged surface [7,8]. Upon measuring, the N, P-CDs recorded a zeta potential of −41.9 mV, which may be attributed to the negatively charged groups on their surface. The properties of negative surface charge and high QY (89%) make the prepared CDs excellent nanomaterial for bacterial cell detection.

To explore the performance of N, P-CDs to differentiate live and dead bacteria, we chose three gram-positive (G+) bacterial cells: S. aureus, B. subtilies and M. luteus and three gram-negative (G−) bacterial cells: E. coli, S. typhimurium and M. luteus. Dead cells were obtained through heating at 80 °C for 1 h. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images (Fig. 5 ) reveled that the prepared CDs can only react with the dead bacteria and emit colorful fluorescence with different excitation wavelengths. However, there was no visible fluorescence in living cells. These results demonstrate that the prepared CDs can distinguish dead bacterial cells from living ones, regardless of their species, with excellent selectivity and sensibility. For this reason, those CDs could be a promising probe in bacteria viability evaluation.

To further study the performance of prepared CDs in microbial cell viability evaluation, we also tested the CDs in distinguish living and dead fungi. Two cell types of fungi (S. cerevisiae and A. niger) were chosen in this study. Dead cells were obtained with the same method as bacterial cells. Fig. 6 shows the confocal fluorescence images, from which dead fungi were successfully stained by the synthesized CDs with bright fluorescence, while the live cells were not. The results show that N,P-CDs also have an excellent effect in evaluating dead and live fungal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between N, P-CDs and PI on living and dead microbial detection
ce:section.ce:para: Propidium iodide (PI) is a fluorescence dye which can only penetrate damaged membranes of dead cells, so it has an outstanding performance in staining bacterial cells. To further study the microbial live/dead differentiation performance of N, P-CDs and clearly their functional mechanism, CDs and PI were mixed and incubated with mixed microorganism of dead and live cells (S. aureus and S. cerevisiae). Fig. 7 shows that the fluorescence of PI and CDs only existed in dead cells while live S. aureus and yeast were neither stained by N, P-CDs nor PI. The results refect that the prepared CDs can only stain dead microbial cells (both bacteria and fungi), which is the same as the effect of PI. Based on this outcome, we could speculate that N, P-CDs may have the same mechanism as PI that stain dead cells via penetrating damaged cell membranes.

In summary, we prepared a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots (N, P-CDs) via a one-step solvothermal method. The synthesized CDs exhibit excellent stability, good biocompatibility, superior photoluminescent, low cytotoxicity and electronegative charge. More importantly, the properties of negative surface charge and high QY (89.9%) enable the CDs can selectively stain dead microbial cells (bacteria and fungi) by penetrating damaged cell membranes with bright multicolor fluorescence under different excitation wavelength. Compared with the commercial dyes such as PI, which is widely used in bacterial staining, the N, P-CDs have high stability, nontoxic, superior sensitivity and low-cost. Our findings suggest that the successfully prepared CDs could serve as a sensitive fluorescent probe in assessing microbial viability, which will widen the research of microorganism detection.

Xingwang Qie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Minghui Zan: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Software. Li Li: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Ping Gui: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Data curation. Zhimin Chang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Mingfeng Ge: Writing - review & editing. Ruo-Song Wang: Writing - review & editing. Zhenzhen Guo: Writing - review & editing. Wen-Fei Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFF0108600, 2017YFC0211900 and 2016YFF0103800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81771982, 61535010, 8160071152 and 21803075), Key Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. KFZD-SW-210), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BE2015601, BK20181236).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.110987.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Nat. Commun.

Nanoscale

J. Mater. Chem. B

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cationic conjugated polymers for discrimination of microbial pathogens
Adv. Mater.

J. Microbiol. Methods
